skill.name: typescript-best-practices
skill.description: Guides TypeScript work with type-first design, exhaustive handling, runtime validation, typed configuration, and focused tests.
skill.summary: Apply practical TypeScript patterns for safer application code and clearer contracts.
skill.author: 0xBigBoss

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, environment, and cryptography patterns, but review found these are Markdown examples and inline TypeScript references in SKILL.md. No executable scripts, hidden files, prompt injection text, credential exfiltration, or malicious operational behavior were found.

low_findings.title: Markdown TypeScript Examples Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to inline backticks and fenced TypeScript examples in instructional Markdown. SKILL.md contains guidance and sample code, but no runnable script, shell command, or instruction to execute untrusted input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are prose or TypeScript code blocks. There is no file other than SKILL.md and no executable command path.

low_findings.title: Example Network Calls Are Relative API Samples
low_findings.description: The fetch findings are TypeScript examples that call relative application routes and add error handling context. The type-fest URL is a documentation link, not code that sends data externally.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators appear inside Markdown examples and a reference link. No evidence found of automatic outbound requests or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable References Are Configuration Guidance
low_findings.description: The environment findings are examples for validating process.env with Zod and using typed config values. They do not read the evaluator environment or transmit secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are a safe configuration pattern in a Markdown code sample. No evidence found of runtime access by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Semantic False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines describe TypeScript practices such as roles, validation, and module structure. They do not contain weak cryptographic algorithms, host reconnaissance, or system enumeration instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Static patterns were present, but semantic review did not find cryptographic operations or reconnaissance behavior. The affected lines are ordinary TypeScript documentation.

content.value_statement: TypeScript projects often lose safety when types, validation, and configuration drift apart. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for type-first implementation.
actual_capabilities: Recommends type-first workflows for data models, function signatures, and implementation.
Shows patterns for discriminated unions, branded types, literal unions, and explicit optional fields.
Guides exhaustive handling with never checks and contextual error propagation.
Explains runtime validation with Zod schemas, safeParse, transforms, and schema composition.
Describes typed configuration patterns for process.env validation at startup.
Encourages focused tests and small feature-oriented TypeScript modules.
limitations: It is advisory Markdown and does not inspect code automatically.
It does not provide React-specific hook or component guidance beyond recommending a paired skill.
It does not replace project-specific architecture decisions or compiler configuration review.
Its examples are concise and may need adaptation for larger production systems.

use_cases.title: Refactor Service Types
use_cases.description: Turn loose service data into explicit TypeScript contracts with validated boundaries and safer state handling.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Check changes for missing edge cases, weak error handling, and places where exhaustive types would prevent bugs.

use_cases.title: Standardize Project Patterns
use_cases.description: Create shared expectations for strict mode, module size, Zod schemas, and typed configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Type Safety
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small TypeScript change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this TypeScript file for type safety. Focus on explicit types, optional fields, edge cases, and simple improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Design Safer Data Models
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate modeling for application state or API data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me redesign these TypeScript types so invalid states are not representable. Use discriminated unions or branded types where useful.

prompt_templates.title: Add Runtime Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Zod validation strategy for this input boundary. Keep schemas and inferred TypeScript types aligned.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a TypeScript Module
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review across design, errors, and tests.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this TypeScript module for strict typing, exhaustive handling, runtime validation, contextual errors, and missing focused tests.

output_examples.input: A request state type with separate loading, data, and error fields.
output_examples.output: The response recommends a discriminated union so each state has only the fields it can validly contain.

output_examples.input: An API client that trusts response data without validation.
output_examples.output: The response suggests validating the response at the boundary and returning typed parse results or contextual errors.

output_examples.input: Configuration values read from environment variables across many files.
output_examples.output: The response proposes one typed configuration module that validates startup values before the app runs.

best_practices: Define data models and function signatures before implementation details.
Validate untrusted input at boundaries and infer TypeScript types from schemas.
Use exhaustive handling, contextual errors, and focused tests for changed behavior.
anti_patterns: Avoid boolean state bags that allow impossible combinations.
Avoid scattering direct process.env access throughout the codebase.
Avoid swallowing parse errors or returning unvalidated external data.

faq.question: Does this skill run TypeScript tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to apply during TypeScript work.

faq.question: Can it help with JavaScript files?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance can support JavaScript migrations and type-aware refactors, especially when adding TypeScript types.

faq.question: Does it cover React best practices?
faq.answer: Only TypeScript fundamentals. It recommends pairing with a React-specific skill for hooks and component design.

faq.question: Why does it recommend Zod?
faq.answer: Zod keeps runtime validation and inferred TypeScript types close to the same source of truth.

faq.question: Is this useful for strict TypeScript projects?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill emphasizes strict mode, exhaustive checks, explicit edge cases, and typed configuration.

faq.question: Can it replace human code review?
faq.answer: No. It supports review by highlighting common TypeScript risks, but project context still matters.
